question: Why is MockOf<T> better than manually creating mocks with as any?

options.text: When interface T changes, TypeScript shows an error in the mock, preventing mock/real code divergence
MockOf<T> runs faster at runtime due to TypeScript optimizations
options.explanation: MockOf is a type-level construct erased at compilation. Runtime behavior is identical. The advantage is TypeScript detecting mismatches when the interface changes

options.text: as any is forbidden in TypeScript strict mode
options.explanation: as any is allowed in any TypeScript mode. The problem is it disables type checking, and the mock may not match the interface

options.text: MockOf automatically generates implementation of all methods
options.explanation: MockOf only defines types. createMock creates the implementation, but not automatically — defaults must be provided. The main advantage is compile-time interface conformance checking

question: How does DeepPartial<T> differ from the built-in Partial<T> for fixtures?

options.text: DeepPartial recursively makes nested objects optional too, allowing you to override settings.theme without specifying settings.notifications
DeepPartial works with arrays, while Partial doesn't
options.explanation: Partial works with arrays too. The difference is recursiveness: Partial makes only top-level fields optional, while DeepPartial applies at all nesting levels

options.text: DeepPartial automatically merges objects, while Partial replaces them
options.explanation: DeepPartial is just a type. Object merging is done by the deepMerge function. DeepPartial allows typing the override argument as partial at any level

options.text: DeepPartial removes readonly modifiers from nested objects
options.explanation: DeepPartial doesn't remove readonly. It only makes fields optional (adds ?). Removing readonly requires a separate utility type

question: Why should each contract test call clear() at the beginning?

options.text: For test isolation — each test starts with a clean state that doesn't depend on execution order
Because TypeScript requires calling clear() before using StorageContract
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't require calling clear(). This is a test design concern: without clear(), test results may depend on which tests ran earlier

options.text: To save memory when running a large number of tests
options.explanation: Memory savings are not the main goal. The primary reason is isolation: if test A adds data, test B shouldn't see it

options.text: clear() resets the internal call counter of the contract suite
options.explanation: clear() is a StorageContract method that clears the storage. The contract suite has no call counter. The purpose is a clean initial state for each test

question: What do contract tests guarantee when there are multiple implementations of an interface?

options.text: All implementations behave identically for core operations
A new implementation can be substituted for an existing one without changing client code
All implementations have identical performance
options.explanation: Contract tests verify behavior, not performance. MapStorage and ObjectStorage may differ in speed, but both correctly implement the contract

options.text: Behavioral discrepancies are caught before reaching production

question: How does the MockFunction<TArgs, TReturn> type ensure mockReturnValue safety?

options.text: mockReturnValue(value: TReturn) only accepts a value of type TReturn, matching the original method's return type
mockReturnValue checks the value type at runtime using typeof
options.explanation: mockReturnValue doesn't perform runtime checks. Type safety is ensured by TypeScript at compile time through the generic TReturn

options.text: mockReturnValue automatically converts the value to the required type
options.explanation: There is no automatic conversion. If a value of the wrong type is passed, TypeScript will show a compile error

options.text: mockReturnValue uses a type guard to check the value
options.explanation: No type guard is used. Safety is ensured by the generic parameter TReturn, which is fixed when creating the MockFunction
